title: Call stack and event loop model

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Call stack and event loop model; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.claim: The event loop runs one task at a time, drains microtasks at defined checkpoints, and lets rendering occur between suitable tasks.

canonicalExplanation.summary: The event loop runs one task at a time, drains microtasks at defined checkpoints, and lets rendering occur between suitable tasks.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A resolved Promise callback runs after current synchronous code but before a zero-delay timer task. Your goal is to predict logged output order for synchronous calls, promises and timer tasks. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; html document skeleton; Sources, console and debugger; pf abstraction calls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: The event loop runs one task at a time, drains microtasks at defined checkpoints, and lets rendering occur between suitable tasks. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Long tasks block interaction, promise callbacks are microtasks, and timers specify minimum delay rather than exact execution time. This draft therefore rejects: setTimeout(fn,0) runs fn immediately before queued Promise callbacks.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A resolved Promise callback runs after current synchronous code but before a zero-delay timer task.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Current synchronous work finishes first, then microtasks drain before the next timer task.
